entry.title: Re: Error in Outbound Delivery Replication from S4/HANA t...
entry.content: The error message in your screen shot says "Contact SAP". However, my suggestion is to first check the message monitoring in S/4HANA Cloud and LGM to see if the payload failed due to mapping or authorization. If nothing obvious shows up, then yes, you should contact SAP, because this type of “technical issue” message usually indicates a backend problem that requires SAP intervention.

entry.title: Re: SAP CPI - json value characater gets escaped when con...
entry.content:
If you are using standard JSON to XML converter, then it will escape characters like < or & to keep the xml valid. To undo this I'm using groovy script right after the converter to replace them to raw form. 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {
def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
body = body.replaceAll("<", "<").replaceAll("&", "&")
message.setBody(body)
return message;
}

entry.title: Re: How to force measurement point entry in PM order conf...
entry.content: No standard way to force measurement entry during PM order confirmation or completion. You can encourage it via status profiles or completion checks, but enforcement requires a custom enhancement (BAdI/user exit). This is why you have not found a delivered option; it is not overlooked, it is by design.

entry.title: What is variant configuration and when should it be used?
entry.content:
If you’ve ever watched a sales rep, an engineer, and a production planner debate the “right” version of a product like it’s the final round of a quiz show, this post is for you. We’re about to demystify SAP Advanced Variant Configuration—the not‑so‑secret weapon that turns a jungle of options into a calm, reliable ordering and manufacturing flow. Grab your coffee (configured to taste), and let’s make sense of complexity without making your master data cry.

 

What is Variant Configuration (and When Should You Use It)?

If you’ve ever tried to order coffee with more than three modifiers—extra hot, half-caf, oat milk, two pumps hazelnut—you already understand product complexity. Now imagine doing that with forklifts, machinery, or electronics and trying to keep your master data from bursting into tears. That’s where SAP Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) comes in: it’s the difference between a chaotic spreadsheet of doom and a tidy, rules-driven model that says, “Don’t worry, I’ve got this.”

 

The Big Idea (No Bullet Points, Promise)

Advanced Variant Configuration lets you represent countless product possibilities using one configurable material. Instead of creating a separate material for every “diesel + 45 performance + 3‑ton + blue + camera” combo, you define characteristics (like engine type, performance, tonnage, color, and accessories), group them logically, and wire them together with rules that keep things sensible. You also build a Super BOM that contains all potential components and a Super Routing with all potential operations. The system then does the heavy lifting: given a customer’s selection, it picks the right parts and steps automatically. You keep your master data lean, your sales team fast, and your production team off your back.

How It Works (In Real Life)

It starts with modeling. You create a configurable material, define the characteristics your customers care about, and organize them into a class made for configuration. You then build one master bill of materials—the Super BOM—that includes every component any variant might need, and a routing that covers all possible operations. Finally, you add object dependencies—rules that clarify what goes with what, so “electric + 55 performance + 5‑ton” doesn’t sneak past quality like a cat through an open door.

When a sales order comes in, the salesperson selects the values the customer wants. SAP looks at those choices, applies the rules, explodes the Super BOM, and produces a variant-specific structure and routing that production can actually build. No guesswork. No “which one did we mean?” Slack threads. Just the right components, every time.

A Forklift Walks Into a Showroom…

Picture a forklift product line with three drive types (diesel, gas, electric), multiple performance levels, tonnage from one to five tons, several colors, and a menu of accessories. The number of combinations balloons fast—into the tens of thousands if you let it. Without AVC, you’d need a material for each variant, plus a BOM and routing for each. Congratulations, you now manage a small library of nearly identical records that differ on line ten, footnote two. With AVC, you model the product once, capture the logic once, and reuse it endlessly. You can even define commonly sold configurations as material variants for stocking or quick quoting, while still relying on the same brains behind the scenes.

When You Should (Absolutely) Use Advanced Variant Configuration

Use AVC when your products are like LEGO sets—modular, customizable, and prone to enthusiastic customers wanting “just one more piece.” If your sales process involves choosing from a menu of features, if your master data threatens to spread like ivy, or if you need to introduce new options without rebuilding everything from scratch, AVC will save your roadmap (and your weekends). It’s especially handy in configure-to-order and engineer-to-order environments, where enforcing valid combinations isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s the difference between smooth production and emergency meetings.

Why Teams End Up Loving It

First, there’s the master data diet: one configurable material instead of thousands. Maintenance becomes manageable, and the risk of copy‑paste errors drops dramatically. Sales gets faster because the system helps them choose valid, buildable options without phoning a friend in engineering. Production gets exactly what was promised, complete with the right components and operations. And finance appreciates that configuration rules can gently steer choices toward cost‑appropriate combinations. In short, AVC keeps everyone aligned, which, in most companies, is rarer than a quiet inbox.

What You Need in Place

Success with AVC starts with a clear, honest picture of your product. That means well-defined characteristics that reflect how you actually sell and build, not how someone wished you did two reorgs ago. Your Super BOM and Super Routing should be comprehensive and well-structured, so dependencies can do their job cleanly. Each configurable material needs a configuration profile to control behavior, and your team should be comfortable with the Product Modeling Environment (PME/PMEVC) to maintain logic. Finally, make sure your modelers speak the language of dependencies—preconditions, selection conditions, procedures, and constraints—because that’s how your product rules come alive. If you’re moving to Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC), expect better performance and more modeling power with the same core principles—like upgrading from a trusty bike to an e‑bike that still pedals, but flies uphill.

The Payoff (and a Gentle Nudge)

Advanced Variant Configuration turns option overload into a controlled, elegant model. It keeps your master data slim, your configurations valid, and your lead times realistic. If your product line is already complex—or your roadmap suggests it soon will be—AVC is the practical way to offer customers choice without breeding chaos. A great way to start is to pick one product line, define the must-have characteristics, draft a Super BOM that mirrors reality, and pilot it with a few real orders. Iterate, tighten the rules, and watch the noise go down while speed and confidence go up.

 

If you’ve made it this far, congrats—you now speak fluent “configurable product.” The next best step is small and practical: pick one product line, sketch the must‑have characteristics, and let a pilot model prove the value. From there, you’ll refine the rules, trim the noise, and watch quoting and production get measurably smoother. May your dependencies be tidy, your BOMs be super, and your lead times politely short

entry.title: Incomplete date handling - are incomplete dates converted to nulls when using CSVs via ODBC?
entry.content:
Hello,

I am having an issue where incomplete dates, e.g. "2025" are evaluated as nulls in formulas in Crystal. I am using CSV files accessed via ODBC as the data source as it's the only available method with my software. When I check for nulls on the date field, it comes up as null on incomplete dates. If I enable "Convert database nulls to default" and format it using:
Totext({table_csv.datefield}, "yyyy-MM-dd")
It returns -4714-12-31.

I've been trying to understand why this is occurring and have seen semi-related posts online that seem to indicate this issue is caused by the ODBC driver being unable to parse incomplete dates, so Crystal sees them as nulls. Is anyone able to confirm if this is correct?

I think my solution will be to force the dates to strings on export from the database, but would be good to understand this potential limitation.

Thanks

entry.title: Regarding Collecting Bills of Exchange Receivable (App ID: F-34)
entry.content:

The help section below describes how to post the collection of bills of exchange using F-34.
(The Japanese test script included procedures for “Presenting Bills of Exchange Receivable,” but did not mention posting the collection.)

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b8c08e0197454541a11f8d46ef1ab96e/1b52272c4d1d402c9aeb70dd3bcee133.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Re: Seeking advice: SAP Generative AI Developer - Hands-on access and Python prerequisites
entry.content:
Hi,

We are glad that you find the learn journey useful. Let me answer your questions one by one:

  1. The learning journey includes a hands on practice  system setup specifically for this learning  journey and the certification, Hands-on Practice for Solving your business problems using prompts and LLMs in SAP's Generative AI Hub. You don't need to setup the BTP Global Enterprise account just to practice the exercises in this learning journey. You can do that if you want to experiment with more models, or develop a pilot and working generative AI solution.
  2. No, there is no Python course by SAP.  However, code in this learning journey is presented in a simple manner with explanations. The focus is on problem solving skills instead of coding knowledge in a particular language. I wish you all the best for the learning  journey.

entry.title: Connecting custom Joule Agents to MCP servers: A POC Architecture for Enterprise HR Intelligence
entry.content:
Building a Custom Joule Agent with MCP on SAP BTP

Most enterprise AI initiatives struggle not because of models, but because of data fragmentation and lack of controlled access across systems. The real challenge is not "chat with your data." It is enabling AI systems to reason across multiple systems — safely, explainably, and in a governed way.

In this blog, I walk through a reference architecture for a POC where a custom Joule agent created with Joule Studio integrates:

  • Curated HR data stored in HANA Cloud
  • Talent management data stored in an external system
  • A governed Model Context Protocol (MCP) server layer
  • A custom query service
  • SAP BTP environment (Cloud Foundry)

The Use Case

Imagine yourself as the VP of Engineering at a large software company. The company is undertaking a major cloud migration project. As the VP, you need to understand the skill landscape of your organization to make informed decisions about hiring and upskilling initiatives.

Today, answering these questions would typically require multiple teams, manual data extraction, and several days to weeks of turnaround time. Skills data lives in an external system. Core employee data lives in SuccessFactors. Insights require joins across both, and responses must be grounded and explainable.

This is where a custom Joule agent + an MCP server becomes powerful.

Concretely, the VP needs to ask questions like:

  • "Which engineers in the Product Development team have Kubernetes experience backed by internal project evidence?"
  • "Show the existing skill gaps we have for the cloud migration project."
  • "Recommend 5 engineers suitable for leading the cloud migration project, and who has related skills and can potentially be upskilled."

These questions are complex. They require data from the external talent management system integrated with master HR data, some level of reasoning, and domain knowledge about organizational structure. Standard Joule capabilities will not handle this effectively, as they require knowledge of the external system's data model. 

The Demo


The Solution: Architecture Overview

Figure 1 — High-level architecture: Joule Studio → MCP Server → Query App → Data Sources


Data Layer

Figure 2 — Data layer: synthetic HR data in HANA Cloud, talent/skills data in PostgreSQL

Since we did not have access to a SuccessFactors instance, we created a data generation library that produced realistic HR data in the style of SuccessFactors with clear reporting structures, departments, roles, and organizational assignments for each employee in a fictional organization. The generated data was then imported into HANA Cloud and consisted of 8 relational tables.

For the external talent management data, another library was created to synthesize employee skills data. The generated skills data was linked to the master HR data from the previous step to ensure downstream integration. A PostgreSQL instance was created on AWS EC2, replicating the external system scenario.

Why synthetic data design matters

Synthetic data must be structurally believable. If relationships, distributions, and cardinalities are unrealistic, LLM reasoning becomes trivial and the demo loses credibility. Our generated data includes:

  • Skill frequency skewed to realistic distributions
  • Proficiency levels assigned probabilistically
  • Evidence count tied to proficiency
  • Full referential integrity across HR and talent tables

This ensures the LLM encounters realistic ambiguity rather than artificially clean data. When the agent searches for cloud migration candidates, it faces partial matches, missing evidence, and competing skill profiles — just as it would in a real enterprise.


Query App Layer

Instead of letting the LLM write raw SQL against HANA Cloud and PostgreSQL, we introduced a query application layer that sits between the MCP server and the databases. This is one of the most important architectural decisions in the system.

Why a query app layer?

  • Prevents unsafe or malformed SQL from reaching production databases
  • Encapsulates cross-system joins in well-tested logic
  • Controls the shape and size of responses returned to the LLM
  • Improves reliability and makes tool behaviour deterministic

The query app exposes predefined operations as a FastAPI service. Each operation maps to a specific business question:

OperationPurpose
get_employee_skills(employee_id)Individual skill profile with proficiency and evidence
get_org_skill_summary(org_unit)Org-wide aggregation of skills and gaps
find_candidates_for_project(skill_set, min_proficiency)Multi-criteria candidate search
get_skill_gap_analysis(program_id)Gap analysis against a target skill profile
get_evidence_inventory(employee_id, skill_id)Supporting documentation for a specific skill claim

Example: Multi-Skill AND Search (Relational Division)

One of the more interesting queries demonstrates why the query layer exists. When the VP asks for engineers with both Kubernetes AND SAP integration experience, the system must enforce AND semantics — employees who have all specified skills, not just any one:

SELECT es.employee_id
FROM employee_skill es
WHERE es.skill_id = ANY($1) AND es.proficiency >= $2
GROUP BY es.employee_id
HAVING count(DISTINCT es.skill_id) = $3

The HAVING clause is the key: $3 equals the total number of requested skills. An employee only passes if they have every requested skill at sufficient proficiency. Without it, this would be an OR search. This is not something an LLM should be constructing on the fly against a production database.

Example: Org Hierarchy Traversal (Recursive CTE)

When the VP asks about the Engineering organization, the query must include all descendant org units — not just the top-level node:

 
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
    SELECT org_id FROM org_unit_ref WHERE org_id = $1
    UNION ALL
    SELECT o.org_id FROM org_unit_ref o
    JOIN org_tree t ON o.parent_org_id = t.org_id
)

This walks the parent_org_id chain, collecting all descendant orgs in a single query. The resulting set is then joined to employee_skill for aggregation. These queries are tested, parameterized, and isolated in dedicated query modules — the LLM never sees a table name or connection string.


Designing the MCP Server

You can think of MCP as a contract layer that translates business-safe operations into LLM-accessible tools. The MCP server provides the interface between the Joule agent and the backend systems:

  • Advertises available tools to the LLM
  • Optionally exposes prompt templates for structured queries
  • Provides resources (contextual data the LLM can read)
  • Supplies high-level instructions to guide the LLM's reasoning
  • Listens for MCP client connections from the Joule agent

We used FastMCP (Python) to build the server. The server's instructions provide the cognitive framework for the LLM:

 
mcp = FastMCP(
    "tm-skills",
    instructions=(
        "You have access to a Talent Management Skills API that stores employee "
        "skill profiles, proficiency scores, evidence, and org hierarchy data. "
        "Use the tools to answer HR and talent questions. Employee IDs look like "
        "EMP000001. Org IDs look like ORG030. Skill IDs are numeric (e.g. 1). "
        "Start by browsing the skill catalog (browse_skills) if you need to find "
        "skill IDs by name."
    ),
    host=settings.host,
    port=settings.port,
    lifespan=audit_lifespan,
)

Tools are thin wrappers around the query app's API. Each tool is roughly 5 lines of actual logic:

Example: MCP Tool Definition

 
@mcp.tool()
@audited
async def get_top_experts(
    skill_id: int, min_proficiency: int = 4, limit: int = 10) -> dict:
    """Find the top experts for a specific skill, ranked by proficiency."""
    response = await client.get(
        f"{BASE_URL}/tm/skills/{skill_id}/experts",
        params={"min_proficiency": min_proficiency, "limit": limit},
        headers={"X-API-Key": API_KEY}
    )
    return response.json()

The @audited decorator captures timing, parameters, and success/failure for every invocation. The full server exposes 16 tools, 2 resources, and 3 prompt templates:

MCP toolBackend OperationPurpose
get_employee_skillsQuery App → HANA + PostgresIndividual skill profile
get_top_skillsQuery App → PostgresIdentify strongest competencies
get_org_skill_summaryQuery App → HANA + PostgresOrg-wide skill aggregation
find_candidates_for_projectQuery App → HANA + PostgresMulti-criteria candidate search
get_evidence_inventoryQuery App → PostgresSupporting documentation
browse_skillsQuery App → PostgresBrowse and search the skill catalog

Why MCP instead of direct Joule skills?

At first glance, it may seem simpler to implement these capabilities directly as Joule skills. However, this quickly becomes limiting in enterprise scenarios. MCP provides:

  • Structured tool discovery — the LLM can inspect available tools at runtime
  • Context resources — the database schema and business questions catalog are surfaced alongside tools
  • Prompt templates — standardised input patterns improve multi-step reasoning consistency
  • Decoupled architecture — the MCP server can be versioned and deployed independently of Joule
  • Observability — tool calls can be intercepted, logged, and audited at the protocol level

MCP transforms a set of API calls into an agentic contract between the LLM and the enterprise backend.


Connecting MCP to Joule Studio

Once the MCP server was deployed to Cloud Foundry, connecting it to Joule Studio involved the following steps:

  1. Deploy the MCP server application to Cloud Foundry (separate subaccount)
  2. Create a Destination in the Joule Studio subaccount pointing to the MCP server URL
  3. Configure authentication on the destination (API key injected via URL.headers)
  4. Register the MCP server as a tool provider in the agent configuration
  5. Define agent instructions that guide the LLM on when and how to use each tool

This effectively turns Joule into an MCP client, capable of discovering and invoking external tools through the standard protocol. For further insight into how you can extend Joule with Joule Studio, refer to Rachel's blog here: Joule Studio: Leveraging Agents, Joule Skills, and Document Grounding to extend your use case 

MCP Server Entry Point

The MCP protocol endpoint and REST audit endpoints coexist on the same ASGI process:

 
app = mcp.streamable_http_app()
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=settings.cors_origins.split(","), ...)
uvicorn.run(app, host=settings.host, port=settings.port)

Why Cloud Foundry?

  • Decouples the MCP runtime from Joule's own lifecycle
  • Allows independent scaling and service bindings
  • Aligns with existing BTP enterprise deployment patterns

Kyma would also be a valid choice, especially for event-driven or microservice-heavy designs. For a tool-centric MCP service, Cloud Foundry is well-suited.


Observability: The Differentiator

One of the most valuable aspects of this implementation is the observability layer. This is where the system moves beyond a demo and into enterprise territory.

We built a custom MCP audit dashboard that provides real-time visibility into the agent's behaviour:

  • Which tools were selected and in what order
  • Chained call sequences (multi-step reasoning traces)
  • Parameters passed to each tool
  • Response payload sizes and latency
  • Gaps between calls representing LLM reasoning time (typically 2–5 seconds)

Audit Schema

Every tool invocation is recorded in a local SQLite database using WAL mode for concurrent read/write:

 
CREATE TABLE tool_calls (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp       TEXT    NOT NULL,
    session_id      TEXT,
    tool_name       TEXT    NOT NULL,
    parameters      TEXT,        -- JSON-serialized
    success         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    duration_ms     REAL    NOT NULL
);

Figure 3 — MCP audit dashboard: real-time tool call traces with timing and parameter visibility

AI without observability is a black box. An agent with a tool audit trail is governed intelligence. Stakeholders can see exactly how the agent arrived at an answer, which builds trust and supports compliance requirements.


Security Model

Current implementation

The current setup is intentionally simplified for a POC, but the architecture is designed to evolve into a production-grade security model:

  • API key authentication between Joule (via BTP Destination) and the MCP server
  • API key authentication from MCP server to the query app
  • Separate subaccounts to enforce network isolation

Enterprise upgrade path

Moving this to production-grade security would involve:

  1. Replace API key auth with OAuth2 via XSUAA with principal propagation
  2. Add role-based access control at the tool level (e.g., HR Manager vs. VP-level queries)
  3. Enforce scoped destinations with service-to-service trust
  4. Log all tool invocations to the SAP Audit Log service
  5. Implement mTLS between the MCP server and the query app

Each upgrade can be applied incrementally without rearchitecting the solution. The separation of concerns — Joule → MCP → Query App → Database — means security layers slot in cleanly at each boundary.


Demo Walkthrough

To illustrate the end-to-end flow, here is what happens when the VP asks:

"Find 5 engineers in the Product Development team with Kubernetes and SAP integration experience backed by evidence."
  1. The LLM analyses the question and identifies the need for organizational data and skill data
  2. It calls browse_skills to resolve "Kubernetes" and "SAP integration" to skill IDs from the catalog
  3. It calls get_org_skill_summary to understand the Product Development team's skill landscape
  4. It calls find_candidates_for_project with the target skills and minimum proficiency thresholds
  5. It calls get_evidence_inventory for each shortlisted candidate to retrieve supporting project documentation
  6. The LLM synthesizes a ranked recommendation with proficiency levels, evidence, and organizational context

Figure 4 — End-to-end demo flow: from VP question to ranked candidate recommendation with evidence

This entire sequence happens in seconds, without manual intervention. The final response includes ranked candidates, skill proficiency levels, supporting project evidence, and organizational context. This is not retrieval. This is multi-step reasoning across multiple systems, governed by a controlled tool framework.


Lessons Learned

  1. Controlled query layers outperform free-form LLM SQL

    Encapsulating business logic in a query app layer dramatically improves reliability. The LLM does not need to understand database schemas, and results are consistent and predictable.

  2. Tool granularity shapes reasoning quality

    Tools that are too coarse limit the LLM's reasoning capability. Tools that are too fine-grained lead to excessive chaining and higher latency. Finding the right granularity requires iterating with realistic questions from your target persona.

  3. MCP instructions are the most important code you write

    The instructions provided to the MCP server have a disproportionate impact on how well the LLM selects and chains tools. Clear guidance on data formats, ID conventions, and recommended starting points (e.g., browse the skill catalog first) meaningfully improved response quality.

  4. Tool descriptions drive autonomous selection

    The LLM chooses tools based entirely on their descriptions. Vague descriptions lead to wrong tool choices. Specific, action-oriented descriptions with examples of when to use each tool dramatically improve selection accuracy. The word "verified" in a user's question should trigger get_evidence_backed_candidates, not get_top_experts — this only works if the descriptions are precise.

  5. Observability transforms stakeholder perception

    Showing the toolchain in real time during demos fundamentally changed the conversation. It shifted from "can I trust this AI?" to "how do we scale this?"

  6. Joule's 10KB response limit shapes MCP server design

    Joule Studio imposes a 10KB limit on individual tool responses. Any endpoint that returns large payloads (like a full skill catalog) needs pagination or filtering. This is a general principle for MCP servers: AI models work better with focused, bounded responses than with large data dumps.


When to Use This Pattern

This architecture is well-suited when:

  • Data spans SAP and non-SAP systems
  • Queries require joins or reasoning across system boundaries
  • Governance and auditability are requirements
  • You need explainable, evidence-backed answers
  • You want to decouple AI orchestration from business logic and data access


What's Next

Possible extensions to this reference architecture:

  • Replace synthetic HR data with live SuccessFactors data via OData
  • Add workflow actions such as project assignment recommendations that trigger approval flows
  • Integrate vector search for policy documents and unstructured knowledge bases
  • Add fine-grained, role-based authorization at the individual tool level
  • Deploy the MCP server to Kyma for event-driven architectures
  • Explore A2A (Agent-to-Agent) patterns for multi-domain orchestration


Final Thoughts

By combining Joule Studio with MCP servers, we can create AI agents that are grounded in real data, governed by structured tools, explainable through audit trails, and extensible across system boundaries.

MCP + Joule Studio is one of the first patterns in SAP BTP that makes this shift tangible. The patterns described here are not theoretical — they were built, tested, and demonstrated with a working POC.

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content:
Hi Soumya,
This is a rather complex requirement on FSCM.
I would suggest these steps:
1. You will need to activate credit check on order level in OVAK for all the order types where you want to do the credit check if there are Cement materials.
2. Then you need to control to trigger credit check if there are Cement materials in the order (Per your description you normally trigger check on deliveries, so I assume the order level check is only for the cement materials). You could do this with a No Check routine. This can be assigned in OVA8. You write a check on XVBAP table to lookup whether there are Cement materials. If NO, then set BYPASS-CREDIT_MANAGEMENT = X.
3. You also want to do the credit check with an overdue value different from what is stored on the credit check rule. For this I would suggest to create a copy of the credit check step in the UKM_CHECK_STEP implementation (UKM_CHECK_130) and maintain additional logic. You would need to change the standard check to use your 90 days value for certain scenarios. The credit check step is called in RFC, so the transaction code won't be simply available. You might need to come up with a different condition.
3*. An alternative would be to overwrite the logic where FSCM side determines the check rule to use another one (on which you maintain 90 days for check step 130). CHECK_ACCOUNT (CL_UKM_CREDIT_CHECKER)

Regards,
Edina

entry.title: Re: SAP backup of data for a specific company code
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Currently, there is no such a feature of taking backups for specific data in a table.
You need to extract the corresponding data from a table and back up the outputs at a safe external storage manually.

Please refer to 'EXPORT Statement (Data Import Export)' for details. 
For your information in case of importing the exported data, please also take a look at 'IMPORT Statement (Data Import Export)' in HANA SQL reference guide.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Tax Posting Issue with Sales Tax Payable G/L Account...
entry.content:
Good day @R_Pooja 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the scenario and the app you used, one of the reasons for the issue is triggered by you entering a tax G/L account as a normal invoice line in Create Supplier Invoice. This app expects tax to be determined from the taxable expense or G/L base line. See KBA 3441490 for more insights on the error “Tax entry not possible in this item(FF724)” you received.

Consult these references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/65002458360e1f60e10000000a44147b.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/77c02c58f5809344e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

In your scenario, your first error appears when you enter the tax payable G/L account 22002000 as its own line item and assign a tax code, the system asks for Base Amount because tax calculation logic needs a tax base for tax relevant posting fields.

Your second error appeared because, after you enter the base amount, the system then checks whether tax entry is actually allowed on that item. As describe earlier in KBA 3441490, the error “Tax entry not possible in this item(FF724)” occurs in Public Cloud when tax is being entered on a line that is not intended to carry manual tax entry in that document context.

The reason why “Posting without tax allowed = Yes” does not resolve it is, that setting does not mean a tax account can be used as a normal supplier invoice item for tax posting. If your business requirement is to post a tax payable accounts directly, then you should make use of Post Tax Payables. See the following references KBA 3168742, 2906426 and 3156456 for more insights.

To conclude, in your scenario, the issue is not that the system is missing the base amount only. The main reason for the error is that 22002000 is being treated as a tax payable account, and tax payable accounts should not be entered as manual tax lines in Create Supplier Invoice. Instead, your supplier invoice should contain the actual taxable expense or G/L line, and the system will calculate the tax from that base line using the tax code. If you need a separate tax settlement or transfer posting to a tax payable account, then you should handle that with Post Tax Payables and not manually add the tax payable account as a supplier invoice item.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4de200b26d5d42bbacae69c6bf6b9923/2730396c384443faa8d6339a7dd07f0e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/e71926565e0fdf21e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

In your design, do not send or enter G/L account 22002000 as a separate invoice line in Create Supplier Invoice. Instead, you should:

  1. Post the supplier invoice with the expense line that represents the taxable base.
  2. Maintain the correct tax code on that taxable invoice line.
  3. Let the system calculate and post the tax automatically to the configured tax account in the background.
  4. Use Post Tax Payables only when you need a dedicated tax payable transfer or settlement posting.

 

For your SAP Concur integration, if SAP Concur is sending 22002000 as a separate G/L item into the supplier invoice interface, then you need to review your integration mapping. The tax should normally be transferred as tax code/amount information on the taxable item, not as an additional manual invoice line to the tax payable account.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Project System, Geographical Enablement Framework Integration
entry.content:
We have an integration feature available as of SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. This is tagged as "SAP Geographical Enablement Framework for Project Systems in SAP S/4HANA".

This business function (OPS_PS_GEF) enables the SAP Geographical Enablement Framework for Project Systems in SAP S/4HANA, so that the entities project and WBS element can be extended with geometric attributes and displayed on a map-based UI.

Activation of the business function OPS_PS_GEF enables the default customizing for Project System Geographical Enablement Framework and results in the activation of the following BC sets: PSGEF_GEFV_EXTENSION, PSGEF_GEFVC_FRW_CONFIG, and PSGEF_GEFVC_UI_CONFIG.

Features

Assignment of Geospatial Data to Projects and WBS Elements

  • You can assign geospatial data to projects and WBS elements. This data is processed by the SAP Geographical Enablement Framework.

  • You can select the project or WBS element for which you want to create geospatial data in the Spatial Editor Fiori app and create the geo coordinates.

Searching and Viewing Project and WBS Element

You can search for project and WBS element and display them on a map UI. The SAP Geographical Enablement Framework can display multiple reference and business layers. This allows you to view the geo-enabled PS objects in a business context to simplify access.

Displaying Basic Attributes of PS Objects on a Map UI

When you select a PS object on the map, you can display a set of basic attributes.

More information can be found here Click Here

Regards

Shubham Srivastava

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to connect two XI adapters to the same...
entry.content:
Hi,

you have to decide which is your default integration layer. For all interfaces to the non-default, you have to specify them in transaction SXMSIF. Once it's done you have to create entries in SXMB_ADM for the RUNTIME parameter "IS_URL". The name you have chosen in SXMSIF for declaring the interface it what you have to enter as Subparameter. Then you can specify the alternative URL or DEST: to your secondary integration layer.

BR Helmut

entry.title: Re: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content: Dear Mrs. Edina, Thank you for your detailed guidance. Based on your inputs and further analysis of our system, I would like to confirm my understanding and proposed approach: Current Design Understanding: • Cement business is maintained under Credit Control Area C010 • Other materials (TMT, Wire Rod, etc.) are under different CCA (e.g., C011) • Credit check is currently active only at delivery level (Credit Group 02) Proposed Solution: 1. Activate credit check at Sales Order level via SAP Transaction OVAK o Credit Check = D (Automatic Credit Control) o Credit Group = 01 (Sales Order) 2. Maintain configuration in SAP Transaction OVA8 only for Cement CCA: o C010 + Risk Categories (Z1/Z2/Z3) + Credit Group 01 → for order-level check o Existing C010 + Credit Group 02 remains unchanged for delivery (No entries will be maintained for Credit Group 01 for other CCAs like C011, so order-level check will not trigger for non-cement materials.) 3. Implement enhancement using UKM_CHECK_STEP: o If Division = 15 (Cement) and oldest open item > 90 days → block Sales Order o Otherwise, allow order creation o Delivery-level checks (180 days) will continue as per existing configuration ________________________________________ Clarification Required: 1. Is this approach correct and aligned with best practices in S/4HANA FSCM? 2. In the BAdI implementation: o Should we enhance the standard “Oldest Open Item” step (e.g., Step 110/130), or o Is it recommended to create a custom check step for this logic? 3. Since we are not maintaining Credit Group 01 for other CCAs, can we safely avoid using a requirement routine in OVA8? Please let me know if any correction is required in the above approach.

entry.title: Re: Required Automation Of Delivery & PGI Creation an...
entry.content:
Good day @Ashish13 

Thank you for your question. I will dissect your described scenario into questions provided below.

Question: Is the Automatic Delivery and PGI creation in SAP Public Cloud?

Answer: Yes, to a degree. In Public Cloud, outbound deliveries can be created from sales orders either manually or automatically when you use worklists.

Question: Is automatic PGI possible?

Answer: Yes, but it is tied to the outbound delivery process, not to a custom QR scanning submission flow.

Question: Is automatic invoice creation possible after delivery and PGI?

Answer: Yes, but it is typically executed through the standard billing flow and billing due list process. In other words, standard invoice creation is available after logistics steps are complete.

Note that India eInvoice and eWay Bill are handled through Document and Reporting Compliance, not as a built in QR scan to trip sheet automation flow. See KBA 3692370 on eInvoice and E-WayBill scenario.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/634261119fec4d58970471f2c4a9a740/3ffb4743aa37475e86ee2e0abcce1daa.html?locale=en-US

The specific flow you described is not available as a single standard delivered process in Public Cloud. The standard Public Cloud supports the core logistics and billing objects, but the QR based scanning flow, custom reference orchestration, consolidated bill package download by vehicle, and trip sheet generation are specific to your customer requirements, and you would need to do a custom extension or a side by side solution outside the Public Cloud scope.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Calculation View Features of 2026 QRC1
entry.content:
Within the time frame of 2026 Q1, several new calculation view features have been released in SAP Business Application Studio when connected to SAP HANA Cloud database QRC1. Some of these features are highlighted below. You can find examples that illustrate the individual features here. An overview of features of other releases can be found here.

 

Reuse of Existing Calculation Views in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)

To reuse existing calculation views in the context of BDC, calculation views can be semantically onboarded. This offers the opportunity to leverage calculation views in SAP HANA Cloud standalone and at the same time to also use and extend the calculation views in BDC. This way the best of both solutions can be used.

Semantic onboarding transfers the semantic and logic of the calculation view without moving the source data into BDC. It creates BDC proxies ready for use in BDC modeling.
 

Semantic Onboarding

1. Start the semantic onboarding process in BDC by selecting a BDC Integration mode in SAP Business Application Studio:
 
Integration Mode
  

 

 

"Flat View": This mode onboards calculation views as a flat structure that includes the private and shared attributes of the calculation views. This integration mode is not available for Dimension Views
Use this option if you do not anticipate larger enhancements in BDC and are looking for a quick integration
   
"Construction Kit": This mode imports only the private attributes of cubes giving you the freedom to recombine or change the structure of the original calculation views in BDC. Also Dimension Views can be imported in this mode.
Use the Construction Kit approach if you require flexibility in recombining calculation views in BDC
 
All integration modes create BDC proxies for the calculation views. Queries targeted to these proxies will be directed to the source calculation view.
 
2. Continue with the semantic onboarding in BDC (requires upgraded version of SAP Datasphere. Below are lab-preview screenshots of what is planned to become available end of April 2026)
: 
 

 


 

Onboarded Semantic

Currently, the following semantic information of calculation views is reused by the Analytic Model:
  • Input Parameters
  • Variables
  • Currency and unit conversion information
  • Labels for user-friendly displays
  • Shared hierarchies for dimensional analysis
  • Information about Dimensions of star-joins
Use semantic onboarding to leverage existing calculation views in the context of SAP Business Data Cloud

Data Classification

Different data classifications can be assigned to calculation views to indicate how data that are returned by a calculation view should be treated.
 

Setting Data Classification of Calculation View

Assign data classification levels to your calculation views to clearly indicate how the returned data should be handled. Simply define the classification under "View Properties". The available ratings are sourced from the _SYS_BI.BIMC_SENSITIVITY_CLASSIFICATION table.
 

The data classification of a calculation view can be read from column SENSITIVITY_CLASSIFICATION of table  _SYS_BI.BIMC_ALL_AUTHORIZED_CUBES

 

When a calculation view is opened or a Data Source added a warning is shown if the current calculation view has a lower classification than any of the directly consumed calculation views.  You’ll be offered the option to upgrade the rating to match the highest sensitivity level of the directly consumed calculation views, ensuring consistent data governance.
 
Use data classifications to support the consistent handling of classified data throughout your company

MDS Cube: Incremental Loading

Use incremental loading for your MDS Cubes - a smart approach that updates only newly added records since the last load, dramatically reducing both processing time and resource consumption.

 
How to enable it
Define an MDS Cube attribute as an Incremental Column:


The selected column must increase with each record that is added to the result set of the calculation view.

During incremental loading only records are loaded that have higher values in this column than the highest value at the last time of loading.

Use the Update command to start incremental loading.
 
Use incremental update when loading MDS Cubes to speed up loading and to reduce CPU and memory consumption
 

Copy Nodes with Descendants

 
Need to replicate complex calculation logic across multiple views? The "Copy with Descendants" feature makes it simple.
 
1. Right-click the node you want to copy with its feeding structures and select "Copy with Descendants" :

2.  In the target view right-click an empty space and select "Paste":

 

 
Your entire node structure, including all feeding components, is duplicated saving you valuable development time.
 
Leverage "Copy with Descendants" to reuse calculation view logic across calculation views
 

Working with Data Sources

Easily copy element texts such as the data source name or calculation view name using a right-click or a keyboard short-cut:

 

Data sources are now included in the Outline panel giving you an instant overview of which sources are used throughout your calculation view and exactly where they appear:

 


 

 
Use the Outline to get an overview of which data sources are used in a calculation view and in which nodes
 

Key Column Indicator

Visual key column indicators help you quickly identify which columns belong to a data source’s key - essential information when defining joins or cardinality information.
 
The information of the key indicator is based on the primary key definition in case of table data sources and on the Semantics key in case of calculation views as data sources. The visual key column indicators are shown in the join definition and mapping displays.

A key indicator can appear in gold or grey and can be solid or dashed:
 
A golden icon visualizes a complete key. All key columns are present.

 
A dashed icon indicates an incomplete key. Only some columns from a multi-column key are projected.

 

Grey indicates that some intermediate operations (joins, unions, or table functions) potentially removed the key property.

 

Use information about key columns e.g., when deciding on join columns
 
 

Spill To Disk Hints in Execution Hints List

 For queries where performance isn’t critical, Spill To Disk hints let you trade execution speed for lower memory consumption. These hints are now conveniently available in the pre-defined hint list:

Decrease memory consumption for queries that are not performance critical using the Spill To Disk Hints
 

Visualize Blockers of Filter Push-Down

Filters typically work best when applied early in query execution, reducing record volumes from the start. However, certain node types and modeling patterns can block filter push-down due to their impact on results.  Dedicated options exist to enforce the push-down of filters also in this case.


Nodes or modeling patterns that block the push-down of filters are now marked by a icon so that it becomes easier to detect and deal with filter blocking operations.
 
Below is an example where a Rank node blocks filter push-down:

 
If you enable the filter push-down the icon disappears:
 

Similarly for multi-consumer patterns:


 

 

Quickly identify potential blockers for filter push down and decide based on your business needs whether to remove the blockers or not

entry.title: S/4 HANA EAM - Maintenance order - Display PRT original document assigned to operation
entry.content:
Greetings,

we are looking for the easiest way to display original files assigned to document linked to maintenance order via production resources and tools (PRT). These PRTs can be linked directly to maintenance order operation or are linked to a task list operation (for preventive maintenance scenarios).  

We would like to offer our maintenance personel the option to review maintenance instructions before  or during exection of maintenance tasks.

So far we have tried the following:

  • Find maintenance orders and operations app - F2173
  • Perform maintenance jobs app - F5104A
  • GUI transactions - IW33, IW32, IW41, IW42, IW44, ....

In the examples we have tried there is no direct way to display the original - first we need to display the document info record (DIR) and from it open the original.  From Fiori apps there is even no direct link to DIR we have to copy paste DIR number to Manage documents app.

Additionaly we are looking for a way to display documents linked directly to maintenance order reference technical object (EQ or FL)  - similar to what is already available in GUI. 

Thank you for your help.

Best regards, M.

entry.title: Re: Adding row ME51N
entry.content:
The issue can be resolved by enabling the option "Item overview as grid control" through:

Personal Settings  Basic Settings  Item Overview
Then activate the checkbox:

Item overview as grid control
After enabling this option, the Item Overview is displayed as a Grid (ALV format) instead of the traditional table control, which provides:

Dynamic display of more rows
Easier scrolling and navigation
Improved usability for handling multiple items
Better flexibility in layout and data handling
Important Note
This setting is user-specific, meaning each user needs to activate it individually unless it is standardized through user guidelines or system-wide configuration

entry.title: % Calculation in Fiori OVP foorplan-Card with global filter
entry.content:
Dear Experts

I need to show the percentage of non-managed spend against the total spend.
The data comes from the Fiori app F0571, 'Non-Managed Spend'.


I reuse the CDS view C_NonMngdPurchasingSpend3 to get the amounts and use them for the card CDS view definition.


The overview has a global filter on supplier, and this is where my issue starts. The percentage calculation works fine without the 'Supplier' filter in the CDS view. However, when I filter first on supplier and then the percentage on the filtered results doesn't work.
I am also unable to set @analytics.query:true to take advantage of @DefaultAggregation. #FORMULA to force the percentage calculation after filtering. I have tried a couple of other solutions, but nothing is working correctly.


Does anyone have any ideas or solutions for calculating percentage values and global filtering on an overview page?

Thanks for any good idea

Christian

entry.title: Run the SAC Multi Action public API using OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion workflow
entry.content:
This article demonstrates how to run the SAC Multi Action public API using the OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion workflow through an example that combines Postman and the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Before reading this article, please ensure you have read another article titled: Introduction to SAC Multi-Actions Public API at https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introduction-of-sac-multi-actions-public-api/ba-p/13577173, as this article serves as a supplement to it.

Run Multi Action public API
with OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion workflow only

This article outlines the OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion workflow using Postman and SAP BTP Cockpit.
In this article, the Multi Action public API will be referred to as MA API, SAP BTP Cockpit as BTP, and SAP Analytics Cloud as SAC.

1. Add a New OAuth Client for SAML, Go to SAC -> System -> Administration -> App Integration -> Add an OAuth Client.

New OAuth Client

After clicking "Add," please save the OAuth Client ID and Secret for use later in step 2.

OAuth Client Information

2. Create a Destination in the Subaccount of BTP Cockpit, Go to BPT Cockpit -> Subaccount -> Connectivity -> Destinations.

Destination configuration is as below:

  • URL: SAC tenant URL
  • Authentication: OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
  • Audience: OAuth2SAML Audience from the “App Integration” tab
  • AuthnContextClassRef: urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PreviousSession
  • Client Key: OAuth Client ID
  • Token Service URL: OAuth2SAML Token URL from the “App Integration” tab
  • Token Service User: OAuth Client ID
  • Token Service Password: Secret of the OAuth Client ID
  • Additional Properties: add “SystemUser”, which will be the business user making the call to MA API.

(The system user is a special user ID that is hardcoded in your destination as value of the SystemUser property. If you set this property, its value is used as the propagated user ID. Refer to help document link:  https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/user-propagation-via-saml-2-0-bearer-assertion-flow#automated-access-token-retrieval  )
(When adding properties, the input box has Auto Complete functionality. You need to copy and paste “SystemUser” into the input box)
(Regarding the plan to phase out SystemUser: yes, we have consulted with the BTP team about this because we've received some concerns.   Currently, BTP team does not have a replacement solution for SystemUser in the SAML Bearer Assertion workflow, so SystemUser will continue to be used for this purpose. SystemUser will be supported until an alternative solution is provided.)

Destination Configuration

3. Create an Instance of Destination Service, Go to BTP Cockpit -> Services -> Instances and Subscriptions -> Create.

  • Basic Info tab:
    • Service: Destination Service
    • Plan: lite
    • Runtime Environment: Other
  • Parameters tab: use the default value

New Instance or Subscription

4. Create a Service Binding for this instance.

New Bingding

5. Download IDP Metadata, IDP Metadata includes entityID and X509Certificate, which will be used later.

Download IdP

View IdP Metadata

6. Add a Trusted Identity Provider, Go to SAC -> System -> Administration -> App Integration -> Add a Trusted Identity Provider.

  • Provider Name: entityID from IDP Metadata
  • Signing Certificate: X509Certificate from IDP Metadata

Trusted IdP

7. Get an Access Token to access the Destination service REST API, Refer to help document link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/calling-destination-service-rest-api#2.-get-credentials 

7.1. Get Credentials for the Destination service.

Credentials of Destination instance

7.2. Use Postman to acquire the access token using the credentials, Go to Postman -> New Collection -> Add POST request, Refer to help document link:  https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/calling-destination-service-rest-api#3.-get-an-accesstoken 

7.2.1. Add a POST request:

  • POST URL: <url>/oauth/token, (The URL is url of Credential from the previous step.)
  • Body: is x-www-form-urlencoded
  • grant_type: client_credentials
  • client_id: Credential clientid from the previous step
  • client_secret: Credential clientsecret from the previous step

Header for Get Token

7.2.2. Send, and you will obtain the access token for accessing the Destination service REST API.

Get Token From XSUAA

8. Call the Destination Service REST API, Refer to help document link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/calling-destination-service-rest-api#4.-call-the-destination-service-rest-api  and https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/consuming-destination-service#call-the-destination-service 

8.1. Add a GET request:

  • uri: uri of Credential of 7.1 step
  • <destination-name>: The destination name created in step2
  • Auth Type: Bearer Token
  • Token: Value from the previous step

URL for get Token

8.2. Send, and you will get the authentication token for accessing SAC.

Got token

9. Get csrf token for calling MA API.

9.1. Add a GET request, fill in the csrf URL and it’s Header, Refer to help document link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/14cac91febef464dbb1efce20e3f1613/bbf348fa4cf64f82889ff0d9efb67cdb.html#api-usage 

Get CSRF

9.2. Copy the token to this GET request (token from step 8.2)

Copy Token

9.3. Send and retrieve the csrf token.

Got x-csrf-token

10 Call MA API.

10.1. Add a POST request, and copy the token to this request (token from step 8.2)

Add token For POST

10.2. Fill in the API URL and add the header, Refer to help document link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/14cac91febef464dbb1efce20e3f1613/80680a8a1ca4460caad7f54675abc091.html 

10.2.1 Open the target multi action in SAC, and copy its “Starter API URL”.

Starter API URL

10.2.2. Fill in the URL with the copied URL and add headers with x-csrf-token’ value from step 9.3

POST URL

10.3. Fill in the body as Help Document says. This sample multi action does not have parameters.

Fill Body

10.4. Send, and you will receive a return value that includes "executionID"

Post Request to MA

11. Query the status of running this multi action.

11.1. Add a GET request and copy the token to this request (token from step 8.2)

Query status

11.2. Copy the Polling API URL.

Polling API URL

11.3. Fill in the URL with the copied URL and replace <executionID> with the value from step 10.4

Query URL

11.4. Send, and you will get the status

Send Query

11.5. The job monitor will log this run. Please check it, REST API indicates the call was made from the MA API.

MA Job monitor

In this article, we walked through the complete steps to run the SAC Multi Action public API using the OAuth 2.0 SAML
Bearer Assertion workflow. The process involves:

1. Setting up OAuth authentication - creating an OAuth client in SAC and configuring an OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion destination in  the SAP BTP Cockpit.
2. Establishing trust - creating a Destination Service instance, downloading the IDP metadata, and adding a Trusted Identity Provider in SAC.
3. Acquiring tokens - obtaining an access token for the Destination Service REST API, then using it to retrieve the authentication token for SAC, along with the required CSRF token.
4. Executing the Multi Action API - calling the MA API with the proper tokens and monitoring the execution status through the Polling API.

By following these steps with Postman and the SAP BTP Cockpit, you can successfully trigger Multi Actions programmatically via the OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion flow.

entry.title: The Data Ownership dilemma — and how SAP Business Data Cloud Data Products helps resolve It
entry.content:
The core problem: Data belongs to the Business, but IT Manages it

Many enterprises agree in principle that “data is a business asset.” The business generates the data, uses it daily, and relies on it for decision‑making. Yet, in practice, IT manages most of the systems, pipelines, and storage, creating a structural contradiction:

  • Business claims ownership, but
  • IT controls the handling.

This leads to a classic accountability gap:
Business expects IT to provide clean, reliable, well-modeled data, while IT expects business units to define rules, definitions, and quality expectations. The misunderstanding is structural.

When Data is copied Into a Data Platform, the gap widens

As soon as data is replicated into a platform such as a data lake, data warehouse, the conversation changes:

  • The business often assumes that data copied into the platform becomes IT’s responsibility entirely.
  • IT, however, needs business input to validate transformations, definitions, mappings, and quality checks.

The outcome is predictable:

  1. IT publishes the replicated data, structuring it according to their best understanding.
  2. Business reviews it and complains:
    • “This data is not clean.”
    • “These definitions don’t match our business reality.”
    • “The numbers don’t reconcile with the source data.”

This friction exists because ownership is unclear at every stage: raw data, transformed data, curated views, and analytic models.

The hidden trap: Recreating an ERP inside a Data Platform

One of the most common pitfalls in this context is that all ERP intelligence ends -up being recreated in the data platform.
Why does this happen?
When business users review data in the data platform, they often compare results with ERP transactions. Any deviation is seen as an error. This pushes IT teams to:

  • Rebuild ERP logic,
  • Recreate document flows,
  • Mirror every table, relationship, and constraint,
  • Re‑implement ERP-specific business rules.

Recreating ERP logic leads to:

  • Massive data model complexity
  • Fragile pipelines that break when ERP updates occur
  • Endless reconciliation loops
  • Slow delivery cycles
  • High cost and low return

Data ownership must evolve — and the SAP SAP Business Data Cloud platform enable that evolution

The tension between business and IT will not disappear simply with new tooling. But SAP’s Business Data Cloud introduces capabilities that directly address the root causes:

Within SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP provides out-of-the-box, ready to consume predefined data flows and models based on every SAP applications : Data Products. These Data Products carry business semantics, and application context. They are perfectly aligned with the business definition of the data and ready to consume.

That means:

  • Business has access to the very same data than in their applications.
  • Data definitions are explicit.
  • IT handles the platform; business handles meaning.
  • Conflicts reduce because expectations are defined in the product lifecycle, supervised by SAP.

By providing curated, governed Data Products sourced directly from SAP applications, Data Products expose predefined structures and metadata, meaning you’re consuming business-ready, not system-ready, data.

This is how BDC helps addressing the data ownership problem :

Problem

Impact

How SAP BDC Helps

Business claims ownership but delegates responsibility to IT

Misunderstandings, poor quality feedback

Data Products enforce decentralized, domain-based ownership with structured metadata and lifecycle governance

Copying data creates ambiguity over who validates it

IT guesses business logic; business rejects results

Data Products include business semantics and expose data via standard interfaces that business can validate early

Recreating ERP logic in the data platform

Complex, costly, brittle architectures

BDC offers curated, SAP-native data products designed for analytics and AI without reconstructing operational logic

Conclusion: Data ownership is a shared responsibility — with the right tools

Data ownership cannot sit solely with IT or solely with the business. It must be shared and formalized.

SAP SAP Business Data Cloud , through its standardized Data Products, provides a practical way to bridge the gap:

  • Business defines semantics, meaning, and rules.
  • IT manages infrastructure, governance frameworks, and integration.
  • Both collaborate on iterative product lifecycles rather than one‑off pipeline deliveries.

Organizations adopting this model move from blame cycles to collaborative stewardship, unlocking faster delivery, higher trust, and cleaner data.

entry.title: Re: Indeed’s Transition Away from XML Feeds: What SAP SuccessFactors Customers Need to Know
entry.content:
Update from our Indeed meeting for anyone interested:

  • They applied for an extension for us for our XML feed which has been approved, to allow us time to implement the API integration. 
  • They've found an alternative solution to filtering the API query (we're using a default string of text within the JD of the job postings that we want to be excluded from the job board, which I've now set up using Job Profile Builder, and they'll apply a rule their side not to post those ones)
  • We've been flagged as 'fast-track' for the integration work, but we are still in a queue for the Indeed implementation consultant to get in touch.
  • They said it's not mandatory to implement the API integration. When the XML feeds are discontinued, we would have the option to revert back to scraping + apply on company site instead. We'd prefer to go ahead with the integration, as there are obviously benefits for the candidates being able to apply natively on the job board.

entry.title: Re: WE GET ERROR FOR BUSINESS PARTNER IN E DOCUMENT COCKPIT
entry.content:
From your screen shot, what the error means

  • IDWew → This is the internal identifier used in Poland for certain entities (often required for KSeF submissions).
  • NIP number → This is the Polish VAT/tax identification number.

If either is missing in the BP master data, the eDocument cannot be generated or sent.

This is not a system bug but a master data gap. Update the BP master data in Production with the required NIP (and IDWew if applicable). After correction, re‑generate the eDocument in the cockpit.

entry.title: Re: Calculation View Features of 2026 QRC1
entry.content:
Hi Community,

"

Filters typically work best when applied early in query execution, reducing record volumes from the start. However, certain node types and modeling patterns can block filter push-down due to their impact on results.  Dedicated options exist to enforce the push-down of filters also in this case.

 

Nodes or modeling patterns that block the push-down of filters are now marked by a icon so that it becomes easier to detect and deal with filter blocking operations.

"

 

Thanks for your selfless sharing again, it really help us a lot.

🙂

 

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: Re: How to deploy multiple intents of same Fiori app to W...
entry.content:
@saikrishna186 ,

Since probably a year or more ago, SAP added the functionality to allow HTML5/UI5 Fiori apps to have multiple intents (and multiple tiles per intent) in Work Zone Standard. We have been using this and it works fine. This is for pure BTP HTML5 apps, which is the topic of this post.

You just need to include the extra intent navigations in the manifest of the UI5 app. This will then allow you to choose each of those intents from the list of apps in the Work Zone Standard content manager.

I looked at the KBA that you linked, but that wasn't about multiple intents, that was about having multiple values for a single parameter in a target mapping. It also looks to be talking about On-Prem apps being exposed on Work Zone Standard. If you are referring to on-prem Fiori apps with multiple intents, I can't confirm that as we don't have that use case currently. All of our apps with multiple intents are local BTP HTML5 apps.

Brendan

entry.title: Re: Week 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account enabled with Joule for Developers
French e-invoicing & S/4HANA Cloud: partial vs residual payments for e-reporting?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As part of a project on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, we are preparing for the upcoming French e-invoicing and e-reporting reform .

From what I understand, the regulation does not define a “partial payment” status, but instead requires reporting of payment events (date and amount).

In this context, I have a few questions:

  • Is there a preferred approach in SAP (partial payment vs residual item) to ensure alignment between AR open items and reported payment events?
  • How are partial payments typically handled for e-reporting (multiple payment events for one invoice)?
  • Has SAP provided any official guidance or best practices on this topic?

The goal is to stay fully compliant while keeping a clean-core approach in Public Cloud.

Any feedback or experience would be highly appreciated!

Thanks,
Corinne

entry.title: Re: How to deploy multiple intents of same Fiori app to W...
entry.content:
Hi Brendon,

Good day ! thank you for the response.

Well that's exactly our issue was and raised a case with sap.

Our scenario is:

  • App imported from WebIde to BAS for NEO to CF migration, in manifest crossApplication Inbound we had 2 intents (1 with a tile & other no tile "hidelauncher":true), because for some users we need to be a tile, for some users based on role they can only do cross app navigation into detail page. This is how it was configured in NEO.
  • App deployed to cloud foundry.
  • In WorkZone content explorer we see only 1 app.

We followed sap help doc: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-portal-service/sap-cloud-portal-service-on-cloud-foundry/configuring-multiple-intents-in-app-navigation?locale=en-US

Since we don't see 2 apps in workzone we raised a case with SAP and they responded stating "After checking the issue internally, we found that due to certain restrictions in Work Zone, it is not possible to have multiple intents for the same Fiori application in Work Zone." and this KBA0003585268.

Well if this multiple intents is working for you. Can you help us detailing how you achieved it?

Thank you, Sai

entry.title: Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public & Private Edition
entry.content:
Welcome to the starting point of a new blog series!

In this series, I’d like to take you along on my journey through my very first Public Cloud project. Just like in my previous posts, the focus will once again be on one of my favorite topics: business partners.
Working in the Public Cloud comes with a few special characteristics – some charming, some challenging. And that’s exactly what I want to share with you here.
My experiences are based on releases 2508 and 2602. Especially in the Public Cloud world, things move quickly. By the time you read this, certain features may already look slightly different, new capabilities may have appeared, or old ones may have been refined. I’ll do my best to keep the content as fresh and up to date as possible.
If you notice something important that’s missing or spot a detail that’s outdated or incorrect, feel free to drop a comment. That way, we can consolidate our knowledge and help each other grow.
Let’s begin with the product name, which has gone through (and will go through) a bit of evolution:

until 2026: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition or SAP S/4HANA PEC
from 2026 onward: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (tbc 😉)

A key companion throughout any SAP Cloud journey is SAP Activate. I’ll be using this framework as a reference in this series as well.

SAP Activate in a nutshell:
SAP Activate is a cloud adoption framework that combines solution‑specific best practices, expert guidance, and a clear, phase‑driven approach to simplify and structure your transformation journey.

Official SAP Activate Page SAP Activate | Accelerate SAP Cloud ERP Deployment

Access SAP Activate via Road Map Viewer  Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me

At this point I would like to sort my subsequent linked blogs to corresponding SAP Activate Phase

 

 Solution Scope and Adoption PlanningFit-to-Standard WorkshopsSolution Configuration  
  Fit-to-Standard Workshops Documentation and DesignTest Preparation - Test Scripts for CALM  
  Data Load Preparation   

 

To speak in SAP Activate language, you see under each phase a deliverable. My plan is to create a blog for each of this deliverable and link it here.
Subscribe this blog to keep updated if a new blog is created and linked.
